text: Good morning, my name is Cataldo Doria and I am the Medical Director of the Cancer Center and the Robotic Institute at Capital Health. Capital Health is a beautiful organization in central New Jersey. Today I would like to show you the way I perform a distal pancreatectomy with the splenectomy.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the gastrocolic ligament, accessing the lesser sac and safely sealing the short gastric vessels. The dissection progresses to the tissue between the spleen and stomach. The splenic artery is dissected near its origin from the celiac trunk, and the surgeon prepares to clip the artery, placing two clips on the proximal end and one on the distal end for enhanced safety.

The surgeon uses scissors to divide the splenic artery and begins developing the retropancreatic tunnel for the division of the pancreatic parenchyma. The dissection progresses from the inferior to the superior border of the pancreas, revealing the bluish splenic vein. The hook is used to dissect around the splenic vein, which contains several collaterals that have been clipped. The splenic vein is prepared for clipping, with one clip placed proximally and two distally, before being divided with scissors.

The surgeon completes the division of the splenic vein and continues to enlarge the retropancreatic tunnel under the pancreas. The tunnel appears sufficiently large, but the surgeon opts to expand it further, using umbilical tape for safety.

The surgeon uses an umbilical tape to elevate the pancreas, securing it with a clip to prevent slippage. A mechanical stapler is inserted with guards for protection against leaks, and the pancreatic parenchyma is divided. The vessel sealer is then employed to dissect tissue on the posterior, upper, and lower aspects of the pancreas, sometimes without energy. The spleen is visibly devascularized, and the remaining tissue anchoring it to the retroperitoneum is divided, completing the operation and preparing the specimen for extraction.
